220 # #create returns in such situation. 221 # 222 # If creation failed because of a unique constraint, this method will 223 # assume it encountered a race condition and will try finding the record 224 # once more. If somehow the second find still does not find a record 225 # because a concurrent DELETE happened, it will then raise an 226 # ActiveRecord::RecordNotFound exception. 227 # 228 # Please note this method is not atomic, it runs first a SELECT, 229 # and if there are no results an INSERT is attempted. So if the table 230 # doesn't have a relevant unique constraint it could be the case that 231 # you end up with two or more similar records. 232 def find_or_create_by(attributes, &block) 233 find_by(attributes) || create_or_find_by(attributes, &block) 234 end 235 236 # Like #find_or_create_by, but calls 237 # {create!}[rdoc-ref:Persistence::ClassMethods#create!] so an exception 238 # is raised if the created record is invalid. 239 def find_or_create_by!(attributes, &block) 240 find_by(attributes) || create_or_find_by!(attributes, &block) 241 end 242 243 # Attempts to create a record with the given attributes in a table that has a unique database constraint 244 # on one or several of its columns. If a row already exists with one or several of these 245 # unique constraints, the exception such an insertion would normally raise is caught, 246 # and the existing record with those attributes is found using #find_by!. 247 # 248 # This is similar to #find_or_create_by, but tries to create the record first. As such it is 249 # better suited for cases where the record is most likely not to exist yet. 250 # 251 # There are several drawbacks to #create_or_find_by, though: 252 # 253 # * The underlying table must have the relevant columns defined with unique database constraints. 254 # * A unique constraint violation may be triggered by only one, or at least less than all, 255 # of the given attributes. This means that the subsequent #find_by! may fail to find a 256 # matching record, which will then raise an ActiveRecord::RecordNotFound exception, 257 # rather than a record with the given attributes. 258 # * While we avoid the race condition between SELECT -> INSERT from #find_or_create_by, 259 # we actually have another race condition between INSERT -> SELECT, which can be triggered 260 # if a DELETE between those two statements is run by another client. But for most applications, 261 # that's a significantly less likely condition to hit. 262 # * It relies on exception handling to handle control flow, which may be marginally slower. 263 # * The primary key may auto-increment on each create, even if it fails. This can accelerate 264 # the problem of running out of integers, if the underlying table is still stuck on a primary 265 # key of type int (note: All \Rails apps since 5.1+ have defaulted to bigint, which is not liable 266 # to this problem). 267 # * Columns with unique database constraints should not have uniqueness validations defined, 268 # otherwise #create will fail due to validation errors and #find_by will never be called. 269 # 270 # This method will return a record if all given attributes are covered by unique constraints 271 # (unless the INSERT -> DELETE -> SELECT race condition is triggered), but if creation was attempted 272 # and failed due to validation errors it won't be persisted, you get what #create returns in 273 # such situation. 274 def create_or_find_by(attributes, &block) 275 with_connection do |connection| 276 record = nil 277 transaction(requires_new: true) do 278 record = create(attributes, &block) 279 record._last_transaction_return_status || raise(ActiveRecord::Rollback) 280 end 281 record 282 rescue ActiveRecord::RecordNotUnique 283 if connection.transaction_open? 428 return records.many? if loaded? 429 limited_count > 1 430 end 431 432 # Returns a stable cache key that can be used to identify this query. 433 # The cache key is built with a fingerprint of the SQL query. 434 # 435 # Product.where("name like ?", "%Cosmic Encounter%").cache_key 436 # # => "products/query-1850ab3d302391b85b8693e941286659" 437 # 438 # If ActiveRecord::Base.collection_cache_versioning is turned off, as it was 439 # in \Rails 6.0 and earlier, the cache key will also include a version. 440 # 441 # ActiveRecord::Base.collection_cache_versioning = false 442 # Product.where("name like ?", "%Cosmic Encounter%").cache_key 443 # # => "products/query-1850ab3d302391b85b8693e941286659-1-20150714212553907087000" 444 # 445 # You can also pass a custom timestamp column to fetch the timestamp of the 446 # last updated record. 447 # 448 # Product.where("name like ?", "%Game%").cache_key(:last_reviewed_at) 449 def cache_key(timestamp_column = "updated_at") 450 @cache_keys ||= {} 451 @cache_keys[timestamp_column] ||= model.collection_cache_key(self, timestamp_column) 452 end 453 454 def compute_cache_key(timestamp_column = :updated_at) # :nodoc: 455 query_signature = ActiveSupport::Digest.hexdigest(to_sql) 456 key = "#{model.model_name.cache_key}/query-#{query_signature}" 457 458 if model.collection_cache_versioning 459 key 460 else 461 "#{key}-#{compute_cache_version(timestamp_column)}" 462 end 463 end 464 private :compute_cache_key 465 466 # Returns a cache version that can be used together with the cache key to form 467 # a recyclable caching scheme. When a new record 469 # comes to match the query, or any of the existing records is updated or deleted, 470 # the cache version changes. 471 # 472 # If the collection is loaded, the method will iterate through the records 473 # to generate the timestamp, otherwise it will trigger one SQL query like: 474 # 475 # SELECT COUNT(*), MAX("products"."updated_at") FROM "products" WHERE (name like '%Cosmic Encounter%') 476 def cache_version(timestamp_column = :updated_at) 477 if model.collection_cache_versioning 478 @cache_versions ||= {} 479 @cache_versions[timestamp_column] ||= compute_cache_version(timestamp_column) 480 end 481 end 482 483 def compute_cache_version(timestamp_column) # :nodoc: 484 timestamp_column = timestamp_column.to_s 485 486 if loaded? 487 size = records.size 488 if size > 0 489 timestamp = records.map { |record| record.read_attribute(timestamp_column) }.max 490 end 491 else 492 collection = eager_loading? ? apply_join_dependency : self 493 494 with_connection do |c| 495 column = c.visitor.compile(table[timestamp_column]) 496 select_values = "COUNT(*) AS #{model.adapter_class.quote_column_name("size")}, MAX(%s) AS timestamp" 497 498 if collection.has_limit_or_offset? 499 query = collection.select("#{column} AS collection_cache_key_timestamp") 500 query._select!(table[Arel.star]) if distinct_value && collection.select_values.empty? 501 subquery_alias = "subquery_for_cache_key" 502 subquery_column = "#{subquery_alias}.collection_cache_key_timestamp" 503 arel = query.build_subquery(subquery_alias, select_values % subquery_column) 504 else 505 query = collection.unscope(:order) 506 query.select_values = [select_values % column] 507 arel = query.arel 508 end 509 510 size, timestamp = c.select_rows(arel, nil).first 511 512 if size 513 column_type = model.type_for_attribute(timestamp_column) 514 timestamp = column_type.deserialize(timestamp) 515 else 516 size = 0 517 end 518 end 519 end 520

    43	    def merge!(other, *rest) # :nodoc:

    55	    # Removes the condition(s) specified in +skips+ from the query.
    56	    #
    57	    #   Post.order('id asc').except(:order)                  # removes the order condition
    58	    #   Post.where('id > 10').order('id asc').except(:where) # removes the where condition but keeps the order
    59	    def except(*skips)
    60	      relation_with values.except(*skips)
    61	    end
    62	
    63	    # Keeps only the condition(s) specified in +onlies+ in the query, removing all others.
    64	    #

    66	    #   Post.order('id asc').only(:where, :order) # keeps only the where and order conditions
    67	    def only(*onlies)
    68	      relation_with values.slice(*onlies)
    69	    end

    71	    private
    72	      def relation_with(values)
    73	        result = spawn
    74	        result.instance_variable_set(:@values, values)
    75	        result
    76	      end
    77	  end

40	        #   Post.where(published: false).unscoped.all # Fires "SELECT * FROM posts"

    59	        # Checks if the model has any default scopes. If all_queries

    70	        private
    71	          # Use this macro in your model to set a default scope for all operations on
    72	          # the model.
    73	          #
    74	          #   class Article < ActiveRecord::Base
    75	          #     default_scope { where(published: true) }
    76	          #   end
    77	          #
    78	          #   Article.all
    79	          #   # SELECT * FROM articles WHERE published = true
    80	          #
    81	          # The #default_scope is also applied while creating/building a record.
    82	          # It is not applied while updating or deleting a record.
    83	          #

    85	          #   Article.create.published # => true
    86	          #
    87	          # To apply a #default_scope when updating or deleting a record, add
    88	          # <tt>all_queries: true</tt>:
    89	          #
    90	          #   class Article < ActiveRecord::Base
    91	          #     default_scope -> { where(blog_id: 1) }, all_queries: true
    92	          #   end

    94	          # Applying a default scope to all queries will ensure that records
    95	          # are always queried by the additional conditions. Note that only
    96	          # where clauses apply, as it does not make sense to add order to
    97	          # queries that return a single object by primary key.
    98	          #
    99	          #   Article.find(1).destroy
   100	          #   # DELETE ... FROM `articles` where ID = 1 AND blog_id = 1;
   101	          #
   102	          # (You can also pass any object which responds to +call+ to the
   103	          # +default_scope+ macro, and it will be called when building the
   104	          # default scope.)
   105	          #
   106	          # If you use multiple #default_scope declarations in your model then
   107	          # they will be merged together:
   108	          #
   109	          #   class Article < ActiveRecord::Base
   110	          #     default_scope { where(published: true) }
   111	          #     default_scope { where(rating: 'G') }
   112	          #   end
   113	          #
   114	          #   Article.all
   115	          #   # SELECT * FROM articles WHERE published = true AND rating = 'G'
   116	          #
   117	          # This is also the case with inheritance and module includes where the
   118	          # parent or module defines a #default_scope and the child or including
   119	          # class defines a second one.
   120	          #
   121	          # If you need to do more complex things with a default scope, you can
   122	          # alternatively define it as a class method:
   123	          #
   124	          #   class Article < ActiveRecord::Base
   125	          #     def self.default_scope
   126	          #       # Should return a scope, you can call 'super' here etc.
   127	          #     end
   128	          #   end
   129	          def default_scope(scope = nil, all_queries: nil, &block) # :doc:
   130	            scope = block if block_given?
   131	
   132	            if scope.is_a?(Relation) || !scope.respond_to?(:call)
   133	              raise ArgumentError,
   134	                "Support for calling #default_scope without a block is removed. For example instead " \
   135	                "of `default_scope where(color: 'red')`, please use " \
   136	                "`default_scope { where(color: 'red') }`. (Alternatively you can just redefine " \
   137	                "self.default_scope.)"
   138	            end
   139	
   140	            default_scope = DefaultScope.new(scope, all_queries)
   141	
   142	            self.default_scopes += [default_scope]
   143	          end

   145	          def build_default_scope(relation = relation(), all_queries: nil)
   146	            return if abstract_class?
   147	
   148	            if default_scope_override.nil?
   149	              self.default_scope_override = !Base.is_a?(method(:default_scope).owner)
   150	            end
   151	
   152	            if default_scope_override
   153	              # The user has defined their own default scope method, so call that
   154	              evaluate_default_scope do
   155	                relation.scoping { default_scope }
   156	              end
   157	            elsif default_scopes.any?
   158	              evaluate_default_scope do
   159	                default_scopes.inject(relation) do |combined_scope, scope_obj|
   160	                  if execute_scope?(all_queries, scope_obj)
   161	                    scope = scope_obj.scope.respond_to?(:to_proc) ? scope_obj.scope : scope_obj.scope.method(:call)
   162	
   163	                    combined_scope.instance_exec(&scope) || combined_scope
   164	                  else
   165	                    combined_scope
   166	                  end
   167	                end
   168	              end
   169	            end
   170	          end

   172	          # If all_queries is nil, only execute on select and insert queries.
   173	          #
   174	          # If all_queries is true, check if the default_scope object has
   175	          # all_queries set, then execute on all queries; select, insert, update,
   176	          # delete, and reload.
   177	          def execute_scope?(all_queries, default_scope_obj)
   178	            all_queries.nil? || all_queries && default_scope_obj.all_queries
   179	          end

   181	          def ignore_default_scope?

   183	          end
   184	
   185	          def ignore_default_scope=(ignore)
   186	            ScopeRegistry.set_ignore_default_scope(base_class, ignore)
   187	          end

   190	          # situation where a default scope references a scope which has a default

20	        # You can define a scope that applies to all finders using
    21	        # {default_scope}[rdoc-ref:Scoping::Default::ClassMethods#default_scope].
    22	        def all(all_queries: nil)
    23	          scope = current_scope
    24	
    25	          if scope
    26	            if self == scope.model
    27	              scope.clone
    28	            else
    29	              relation.merge!(scope)
    30	            end

    32	            default_scoped(all_queries: all_queries)

    45	        def default_scoped(scope = relation, all_queries: nil)

    57	        # Adds a class method for retrieving and querying objects.
    58	        # The method is intended to return an ActiveRecord::Relation
    59	        # object, which is composable with other scopes.
    60	        # If it returns +nil+ or +false+, an
    61	        # {all}[rdoc-ref:Scoping::Named::ClassMethods#all] scope is returned instead.
    62	        #
    63	        # A \scope represents a narrowing of a database query, such as
    64	        # <tt>where(color: :red).select('shirts.*').includes(:washing_instructions)</tt>.
    65	        #
    66	        #   class Shirt < ActiveRecord::Base
    67	        #     scope :red, -> { where(color: 'red') }
    68	        #     scope :dry_clean_only, -> { joins(:washing_instructions).where('washing_instructions.dry_clean_only = ?', true) }
    69	        #   end
    70	        #
    71	        # The above calls to #scope define class methods <tt>Shirt.red</tt> and
    72	        # <tt>Shirt.dry_clean_only</tt>. <tt>Shirt.red</tt>, in effect,
    73	        # represents the query <tt>Shirt.where(color: 'red')</tt>.
    74	        #
    75	        # Note that this is simply 'syntactic sugar' for defining an actual
    76	        # class method:
    77	        #
    78	        #   class Shirt < ActiveRecord::Base
    79	        #     def self.red
    80	        #       where(color: 'red')
    81	        #     end
    82	        #   end
    83	        #
    84	        # Unlike <tt>Shirt.find(...)</tt>, however, the object returned by
    85	        # <tt>Shirt.red</tt> is not an Array but an ActiveRecord::Relation,
    86	        # which is composable with other scopes; it resembles the association object
    87	        # constructed by a {has_many}[rdoc-ref:Associations::ClassMethods#has_many]
    88	        # declaration. For instance, you can invoke <tt>Shirt.red.first</tt>, <tt>Shirt.red.count</tt>,
    89	        # <tt>Shirt.red.where(size: 'small')</tt>. Also, just as with the
    90	        # association objects, named \scopes act like an Array, implementing
    91	        # Enumerable; <tt>Shirt.red.each(&block)</tt>, <tt>Shirt.red.first</tt>,
    92	        # and <tt>Shirt.red.inject(memo, &block)</tt> all behave as if
    93	        # <tt>Shirt.red</tt> really was an array.
    94	        #
    95	        # These named \scopes are composable. For instance,
    96	        # <tt>Shirt.red.dry_clean_only</tt> will produce all shirts that are
    97	        # both red and dry clean only. Nested finds and calculations also work
    98	        # with these compositions: <tt>Shirt.red.dry_clean_only.count</tt>
    99	        # returns the number of garments for which these criteria obtain.
   100	        # Similarly with <tt>Shirt.red.dry_clean_only.average(:thread_count)</tt>.
   101	        #
   102	        # All scopes are available as class methods on the ActiveRecord::Base
   103	        # descendant upon which the \scopes were defined. But they are also
   104	        # available to {has_many}[rdoc-ref:Associations::ClassMethods#has_many]
   105	        # associations. If,
   106	        #
   107	        #   class Person < ActiveRecord::Base
   108	        #     has_many :shirts
   109	        #   end
   110	        #
   111	        # then <tt>elton.shirts.red.dry_clean_only</tt> will return all of
   112	        # Elton's red, dry clean only shirts.
   113	        #
   114	        # \Named scopes can also have extensions, just as with
   115	        # {has_many}[rdoc-ref:Associations::ClassMethods#has_many] declarations:
   116	        #
   117	        #   class Shirt < ActiveRecord::Base
   118	        #     scope :red, -> { where(color: 'red') } do
   119	        #       def dom_id
   120	        #         'red_shirts'
   121	        #       end
   122	        #     end
   123	        #   end
   124	        #
   125	        # Scopes can also be used while creating/building a record.
   126	        #
   127	        #   class Article < ActiveRecord::Base
   128	        #     scope :published, -> { where(published: true) }
   129	        #   end
   130	        #
   131	        #   Article.published.new.published    # => true
   132	        #   Article.published.create.published # => true
   133	        #
   134	        # \Class methods on your model are automatically available
   135	        # on scopes. Assuming the following setup:
   136	        #
   137	        #   class Article < ActiveRecord::Base
   138	        #     scope :published, -> { where(published: true) }
   139	        #     scope :featured, -> { where(featured: true) }
   140	        #
   141	        #     def self.latest_article
   142	        #       order('published_at desc').first
   143	        #     end
   144	        #
   145	        #     def self.titles
   146	        #       pluck(:title)
   147	        #     end
   148	        #   end
   149	        #
   150	        # We are able to call the methods like this:
   151	        #
   152	        #   Article.published.featured.latest_article
   153	        #   Article.featured.titles
   154	        def scope(name, body, &block)
   155	          unless body.respond_to?(:call)
   156	            raise ArgumentError, "The scope body needs to be callable."
   157	          end
   158	
   159	          if dangerous_class_method?(name)
   160	            raise ArgumentError, "You tried to define a scope named \"#{name}\" " \
   161	              "on the model \"#{self.name}\", but Active Record already defined " \
   162	              "a class method with the same name."
   163	          end
   164	
   165	          if method_defined_within?(name, Relation)
   166	            raise ArgumentError, "You tried to define a scope named \"#{name}\" " \
   167	              "on the model \"#{self.name}\", but ActiveRecord::Relation already defined " \
   168	              "an instance method with the same name."
   169	          end
   170	
   171	          extension = Module.new(&block) if block
   172	
   173	          if body.respond_to?(:to_proc)
   174	            singleton_class.define_method(name) do |*args|
   175	              scope = all._exec_scope(*args, &body)
   176	              scope = scope.extending(extension) if extension
   177	              scope
   178	            end
   179	          else
   180	            singleton_class.define_method(name) do |*args|
   181	              scope = body.call(*args) || all
   182	              scope = scope.extending(extension) if extension
   183	              scope
   184	            end
   185	          end
   186	          singleton_class.send(:ruby2_keywords, name)
   187	
   188	          generate_relation_method(name)
   189	        end
   190	
   191	        private
   192	          def singleton_method_added(name)
   193	            super
   194	            # Most Kernel extends are both singleton and instance methods so
   195	            # respond_to is a fast check, but we don't want to define methods
   196	            # only on the module (ex. Module#name)
   197	            generate_relation_method(name) if Kernel.respond_to?(name) && (Kernel.method_defined?(name) || Kernel.private_method_defined?(name)) && !ActiveRecord::Relation.method_defined?(name)
   198	          end
   199	      end
   200	    end

    21	      # Applying values to a relation has some side effects. E.g.
    22	      # interpolation might take place for where values. So we should
    23	      # build a relation to merge in rather than directly merging
    24	      # the values.
    25	      def other
    26	        other = Relation.create(
    27	          relation.model,
    28	          table: relation.table,
    29	          predicate_builder: relation.predicate_builder
    30	        )

    32	          k = :_select if k == :select
    33	          if Array === v
    34	            other.public_send("#{k}!", *v)
    35	          else
    36	            other.public_send("#{k}!", v)
    37	          end
    38	        end
    39	        other
    40	      end
    41	    end

    43	    class Merger # :nodoc:
    44	      attr_reader :relation, :values, :other
    45	
    46	      def initialize(relation, other)
    47	        @relation = relation
    48	        @values   = other.values

    50	      end

    52	      NORMAL_VALUES = (Relation::VALUE_METHODS - Relation::CLAUSE_METHODS -
    53	                      [
    54	                        :select, :includes, :preload, :joins, :left_outer_joins,
    55	                        :order, :reverse_order, :lock, :create_with, :reordering

    63	          # `false.blank?` returns `true`, so there needs to be an extra check so that explicit `false` values
    64	          # don't fall through the cracks.
    65	          unless value.nil? || (value.blank? && false != value)

    70	        relation.none! if other.null_relation?

    73	        merge_multi_values
    74	        merge_single_values
    75	        merge_clauses
    76	        merge_preloads
    77	        merge_joins

    95	
    96	        def merge_preloads

   117	        def merge_joins

   136	        def merge_outer_joins

   907	    #   +upsert_all+ will update all the columns that can be updated. These are all the columns
   908	    #   except primary keys, read-only columns, and columns covered by the optional +unique_by+
   909	    #
   910	    #   Example:
   911	    #
   912	    #     Commodity.upsert_all(
   913	    #       [
   914	    #         { id: 2, name: "Copper", price: 4.84 },
   915	    #         { id: 4, name: "Gold", price: 1380.87 },
   916	    #         { id: 6, name: "Aluminium", price: 0.35 }
   917	    #       ],
   918	    #       update_only: [:price] # Only prices will be updated
   919	    #     )
   920	    #
   921	    #   See the related +:on_duplicate+ option. Both options can't be used at the same time.
   922	    #
   923	    # [:record_timestamps]

   925	    #   the model's <tt>record_timestamps</tt> config, matching typical
   926	    #   behavior.

   929	    #   way or the other, pass <tt>:record_timestamps</tt>:
   930	    #

   932	    #     record_timestamps: false # Never set timestamps automatically

   934	    # ==== Examples
   935	    #
   936	    #   # Inserts multiple records, performing an upsert when records have duplicate ISBNs.
   937	    #   # Here "Eloquent Ruby" overwrites "Rework" because its ISBN is duplicate.
   938	    #
   939	    #   Book.upsert_all([
   940	    #     { title: "Rework", author: "David", isbn: "1" },
   941	    #     { title: "Eloquent Ruby", author: "Russ", isbn: "1" }
   942	    #   ], unique_by: :isbn)

   944	    #   Book.find_by(isbn: "1").title # => "Eloquent Ruby"

   949	    # Updates the counters of the records in the current relation.
   950	    #

   964	      updates = {}

   981	    # Touches all records in the current relation, setting the +updated_at+/+updated_on+ attributes to the current time or the time specified.

   989	    #   # Touch all records

   996	    #
   997	    #   # Touch multiple records with a specified time
   998	    #   Person.all.touch_all(time: Time.new(2020, 5, 16, 0, 0, 0))

  1001	    #   # Touch records with scope
  1002	    #   Person.where(name: 'David').touch_all

  1004	    def touch_all(*names, time: nil)
  1005	      update_all model.touch_attributes_with_time(*names, time: time)

  1008	    # Destroys the records by instantiating each
  1009	    # record and calling its {#destroy}[rdoc-ref:Persistence#destroy] method.
  1010	    # Each object's callbacks are executed (including <tt>:dependent</tt> association options).
  1011	    # Returns the collection of objects that were destroyed; each will be frozen, to
  1012	    # reflect that no changes should be made (since they can't be persisted).
  1013	    #
  1014	    # Note: Instantiation, callback execution, and deletion of each
  1015	    # record can be time consuming when you're removing many records at
  1016	    # once. It generates at least one SQL +DELETE+ query per record (or
  1017	    # possibly more, to enforce your callbacks). If you want to delete many
  1018	    # rows quickly, without concern for their associations or callbacks, use
  1019	    # #delete_all instead.
  1020	    #
  1021	    # ==== Examples
  1022	    #
  1023	    #   Person.where(age: 0..18).destroy_all
  1024	    def destroy_all
  1025	      records.each(&:destroy).tap { reset }
  1026	    end
  1027	
  1028	    # Deletes the records without instantiating the records
  1029	    # first, and hence not calling the {#destroy}[rdoc-ref:Persistence#destroy]
  1030	    # method nor invoking callbacks.
  1031	    # This is a single SQL DELETE statement that goes straight to the database, much more
  1032	    # efficient than #destroy_all. Be careful with relations though, in particular
  1033	    # <tt>:dependent</tt> rules defined on associations are not honored. Returns the
  1034	    # number of rows affected.
  1035	    #
  1036	    #   Post.where(person_id: 5).where(category: ['Something', 'Else']).delete_all
  1037	    #
  1038	    # This call deletes the affected posts all at once with a single DELETE statement.
  1039	    # If you need to destroy dependent associations or call your <tt>before_*</tt> or
  1040	    # +after_destroy+ callbacks, use the #destroy_all method instead.
  1041	    #
  1042	    # If an invalid method is supplied, #delete_all raises an ActiveRecordError:
  1043	    #
  1044	    #   Post.distinct.delete_all
  1045	    #   # => ActiveRecord::ActiveRecordError: delete_all doesn't support distinct
  1046	    def delete_all
  1047	      return 0 if @none
  1048	
  1049	      invalid_methods = INVALID_METHODS_FOR_UPDATE_AND_DELETE_ALL.select do |method|
  1050	        value = @values[method]
  1051	        method == :distinct ? value : value&.any?
  1052	      end
  1053	      if invalid_methods.any?
  1054	        raise ActiveRecordError.new("delete_all doesn't support #{invalid_methods.join(', ')}")
  1055	      end
  1056	
  1057	      model.with_connection do |c|
  1058	        arel = eager_loading? ? apply_join_dependency.arel : arel()
  1059	        arel.source.left = table
  1060	
  1061	        key = if model.composite_primary_key?
  1062	          primary_key.map { |pk| table[pk] }
  1063	        else
  1064	          table[primary_key]
  1065	        end
  1066	        stmt = arel.compile_delete(key)
  1067	
  1068	        c.delete(stmt, "#{model} Delete All").tap { reset }
  1069	      end
  1070	    end

  1072	    # Deletes the row with a primary key matching the +id+ argument, using an
  1073	    # SQL +DELETE+ statement, and returns the number of rows deleted. Active
  1074	    # Record objects are not instantiated, so the object's callbacks are not
  1075	    # executed, including any <tt>:dependent</tt> association options.
  1076	    #
  1077	    # You can delete multiple rows at once by passing an Array of <tt>id</tt>s.
  1078	    #
  1079	    # Note: Although it is often much faster than the alternative, #destroy,
  1080	    # skipping callbacks might bypass business logic in your application
  1081	    # that ensures referential integrity or performs other essential jobs.
  1082	    #
  1083	    # ==== Examples
  1084	    #
  1085	    #   # Delete a single row
  1086	    #   Todo.delete(1)
  1087	    #
  1088	    #   # Delete multiple rows
  1089	    #   Todo.delete([2,3,4])
  1090	    def delete(id_or_array)
  1091	      return 0 if id_or_array.nil? || (id_or_array.is_a?(Array) && id_or_array.empty?)
  1092	
  1093	      where(model.primary_key => id_or_array).delete_all
  1094	    end

  1097	    # Destroy an object (or multiple objects) that has the given id. The object is instantiated first,
  1098	    # therefore all callbacks and filters are fired off before the object is deleted. This method is
  1099	    # less efficient than #delete but allows cleanup methods and other actions to be run.
  1100	    #
  1101	    # This essentially finds the object (or multiple objects) with the given id, creates a new object
  1102	    # from the attributes, and then calls destroy on it.
  1103	    #
  1104	    # ==== Parameters
  1105	    #
  1106	    # * +id+ - This should be the id or an array of ids to be destroyed.
  1107	    #
  1108	    # ==== Examples
  1109	    #
  1110	    #   # Destroy a single object
  1111	    #   Todo.destroy(1)
  1112	    #
  1113	    #   # Destroy multiple objects
  1114	    #   todos = [1,2,3]
  1115	    #   Todo.destroy(todos)

     4	module ActiveRecord
     5	  module Delegation # :nodoc:
     6	    class << self
     7	      def delegated_classes
     8	        [
     9	          ActiveRecord::Relation,
    10	          ActiveRecord::Associations::CollectionProxy,
    11	          ActiveRecord::AssociationRelation,
    12	          ActiveRecord::DisableJoinsAssociationRelation,
    13	        ]
    14	      end
    15	
    16	      def uncacheable_methods
    17	        @uncacheable_methods ||= (
    18	          delegated_classes.flat_map(&:public_instance_methods) - ActiveRecord::Relation.public_instance_methods
    19	        ).to_set.freeze
    20	      end
    21	    end
    22	
    23	    module DelegateCache # :nodoc:
    24	      @delegate_base_methods = true
    25	      singleton_class.attr_accessor :delegate_base_methods
    26	
    27	      def relation_delegate_class(klass)
    28	        @relation_delegate_cache[klass]
    29	      end
    30	
    31	      def initialize_relation_delegate_cache
    32	        @relation_delegate_cache = cache = {}
    33	        Delegation.delegated_classes.each do |klass|
    34	          delegate = Class.new(klass) {
    35	            include ClassSpecificRelation
    36	          }
    37	          include_relation_methods(delegate)
    38	          mangled_name = klass.name.gsub("::", "_")
    39	          const_set mangled_name, delegate
    40	          private_constant mangled_name
    41	
    42	          cache[klass] = delegate
    43	        end
    44	        @relation_delegate_cache.freeze
    45	      end
    46	
    47	      def inherited(child_class)
    48	        child_class.initialize_relation_delegate_cache
    49	        super
    50	      end
    51	
    52	      def generate_relation_method(method)
    53	        generated_relation_methods.generate_method(method)
    54	      end
    55	
    56	      protected
    57	        def include_relation_methods(delegate)
    58	          superclass.include_relation_methods(delegate) unless base_class?
    59	          delegate.include generated_relation_methods
    60	        end
    61	
    62	      private
    63	        def generated_relation_methods
    64	          @generated_relation_methods ||= GeneratedRelationMethods.new.tap do |mod|
    65	            const_set(:GeneratedRelationMethods, mod)
    66	            private_constant :GeneratedRelationMethods
    67	          end
    68	        end
    69	    end
    70	
    71	    class GeneratedRelationMethods < Module # :nodoc:
    72	      MUTEX = Mutex.new
    73	
    74	      def generate_method(method)
    75	        MUTEX.synchronize do
    76	          return if method_defined?(method)
    77	
    78	          if /\A[a-zA-Z_]\w*[!?]?\z/.match?(method) && !::ActiveSupport::Delegation::RESERVED_METHOD_NAMES.include?(method.to_s)
    79	            module_eval <<-RUBY, __FILE__, __LINE__ + 1
    80	              def #{method}(...)
    81	                scoping { model.#{method}(...) }
    82	              end
    83	            RUBY
    84	          else
    85	            define_method(method) do |*args, **kwargs, &block|
    86	              scoping { model.public_send(method, *args, **kwargs, &block) }
    87	            end
    88	          end
    89	        end
    90	      end
    91	    end
    92	    private_constant :GeneratedRelationMethods

    94	    extend ActiveSupport::Concern
    95	
    96	    # This module creates compiled delegation methods dynamically at runtime, which makes
    97	    # subsequent calls to that method faster by avoiding method_missing. The delegations
    98	    # may vary depending on the model of a relation, so we create a subclass of Relation
    99	    # for each different model, and the delegations are compiled into that subclass only.
   100	
   101	    delegate :to_xml, :encode_with, :length, :each, :join, :intersect?,
   102	             :[], :&, :|, :+, :-, :sample, :reverse, :rotate, :compact, :in_groups, :in_groups_of,
   103	             :to_sentence, :to_fs, :to_formatted_s, :as_json,
   104	             :shuffle, :split, :slice, :index, :rindex, to: :records
   105	
   106	    delegate :primary_key, :with_connection, :connection, :table_name, :transaction, :sanitize_sql_like, :unscoped, :name, to: :model
   107	
   108	    module ClassSpecificRelation # :nodoc:
   109	      extend ActiveSupport::Concern
   110	
   111	      module ClassMethods # :nodoc:
   112	        def name
   113	          superclass.name
   114	        end
   115	      end
   116	
   117	      private
   118	        def method_missing(method, ...)
   119	          if model.respond_to?(method)
   120	            if !DelegateCache.delegate_base_methods && Base.respond_to?(method)
   121	              # A common mistake in Active Record's own code is to call `ActiveRecord::Base`
   122	              # class methods on Association. It works because it's automatically delegated, but
   123	              # can introduce subtle bugs because it sets the global scope.
   124	              # We can't deprecate this behavior because gems might depend on it, however we
   125	              # can ban it from Active Record's own test suite to avoid regressions.
   126	              raise NotImplementedError, "Active Record code shouldn't rely on association delegation into ActiveRecord::Base methods"
   127	            elsif !Delegation.uncacheable_methods.include?(method)
   128	              model.generate_relation_method(method)
   129	            end
   130	
   131	            scoping { model.public_send(method, ...) }
   132	          else
   133	            super
   134	          end
   135	        end
   136	    end
   137	
   138	    module ClassMethods # :nodoc:
   139	      def create(model, ...)
   140	        relation_class_for(model).new(model, ...)
   141	      end
   142	
   143	      private
   144	        def relation_class_for(model)
   145	          model.relation_delegate_class(self)
   146	        end
   147	    end
   148	
   149	    private
   150	      def respond_to_missing?(method, _)
   151	        super || model.respond_to?(method)
   152	      end
   153	  end
   154	end

90	    #
    91	    # If the relation hasn't been loaded yet, calling +count+ with a block will
    92	    # load all records in the relation. If there are a lot of records in the
    93	    # relation, loading all records could result in performance issues.
    94	    def count(column_name = nil)
    95	      if block_given?
    96	        unless column_name.nil?
    97	          raise ArgumentError, "Column name argument is not supported when a block is passed."
    98	        end
    99	
   100	        super()
   101	      else
   102	        calculate(:count, column_name)
   103	      end
   104	    end
   105	
   106	    # Same as #count, but performs the query asynchronously and returns an
   107	    # ActiveRecord::Promise.
   108	    def async_count(column_name = nil)
   109	      async.count(column_name)
   110	    end
   111	
   112	    # Calculates the average value on a given column. Returns +nil+ if there's
   113	    # no row. See #calculate for examples with options.
   114	    #
   115	    #   Person.average(:age) # => 35.8
   116	    def average(column_name)

   118	    end

   120	    # Same as #average, but performs the query asynchronously and returns an
   121	    # ActiveRecord::Promise.

   124	    end

   126	    # Calculates the minimum value on a given column. The value is returned
   127	    # with the same data type of the column, or +nil+ if there's no row. See
   128	    # #calculate for examples with options.
   129	    #
   130	    #   Person.minimum(:age) # => 7
   131	    def minimum(column_name)
   132	      calculate(:minimum, column_name)
   133	    end
   134	
   135	    # Same as #minimum, but performs the query asynchronously and returns an
   136	    # ActiveRecord::Promise.
   137	    def async_minimum(column_name)
   138	      async.minimum(column_name)
   139	    end
   140	
   141	    # Calculates the maximum value on a given column. The value is returned
   142	    # with the same data type of the column, or +nil+ if there's no row. See
   143	    # #calculate for examples with options.
   144	    #
   145	    #   Person.maximum(:age) # => 93
   146	    def maximum(column_name)
   147	      calculate(:maximum, column_name)
   148	    end
   149	
   150	    # Same as #maximum, but performs the query asynchronously and returns an
   151	    # ActiveRecord::Promise.
   152	    def async_maximum(column_name)
   153	      async.maximum(column_name)
   154	    end
   155	
   156	    # Calculates the sum of values on a given column. The value is returned
   157	    # with the same data type of the column, +0+ if there's no row. See
   158	    # #calculate for examples with options.
   159	    #
   160	    #   Person.sum(:age) # => 4562
   161	    #
   162	    # When given a block, calls the block with each record in the relation and
   163	    # returns the sum of +initial_value_or_column+ plus the block return values:
   164	    #
   165	    #   Person.sum { |person| person.age } # => 4562
   166	    #   Person.sum(1000) { |person| person.age } # => 5562
   167	    #

   169	    # load all records in the relation. If there are a lot of records in the
   170	    # relation, loading all records could result in performance issues.
   171	    def sum(initial_value_or_column = 0, &block)

   173	        map(&block).sum(initial_value_or_column)
   174	      else
   175	        calculate(:sum, initial_value_or_column)
   176	      end
   177	    end
   178	
   179	    # Same as #sum, but performs the query asynchronously and returns an
   180	    # ActiveRecord::Promise.
   181	    def async_sum(identity_or_column = nil)

   183	    end
   184	
   185	    # This calculates aggregate values in the given column. Methods for #count, #sum, #average,
   186	    # #minimum, and #maximum have been added as shortcuts.
   187	    #
   188	    #   Person.calculate(:count, :all) # The same as Person.count
   189	    #   Person.average(:age) # SELECT AVG(age) FROM people...
   190	    #
   191	    #   # Selects the minimum age for any family without any minors
   192	    #   Person.group(:last_name).having("min(age) > 17").minimum(:age)
   193	    #
   194	    #   Person.sum("2 * age")
   195	    #
   196	    # There are two basic forms of output:
   197	    #
   198	    # * Single aggregate value: The single value is type cast to Integer for COUNT, Float
   199	    #   for AVG, and the given column's type for everything else.
   200	    #
   201	    # * Grouped values: This returns an ordered hash of the values and groups them. It
   202	    #   takes either a column name, or the name of a belongs_to association.
   203	    #
   204	    #      values = Person.group('last_name').maximum(:age)
   205	    #      puts values["Drake"]
   206	    #      # => 43
   207	    #
   208	    #      drake  = Family.find_by(last_name: 'Drake')
   209	    #      values = Person.group(:family).maximum(:age) # Person belongs_to :family
   210	    #      puts values[drake]
   211	    #      # => 43
   212	    #
   213	    #      values.each do |family, max_age|
   214	    #        ...
   215	    #      end
   216	    def calculate(operation, column_name)
   217	      operation = operation.to_s.downcase
   218	
   219	      if @none
   220	        case operation
   221	        when "count", "sum"
   222	          result = group_values.any? ? Hash.new : 0
   223	          return @async ? Promise::Complete.new(result) : result
   224	        when "average", "minimum", "maximum"
   225	          result = group_values.any? ? Hash.new : nil
   226	          return @async ? Promise::Complete.new(result) : result
   227	        end
   228	      end
   229	
   230	      if has_include?(column_name)
   231	        relation = apply_join_dependency
   232	
   233	        if operation == "count"
   234	          unless distinct_value || distinct_select?(column_name || select_for_count)
   235	            relation.distinct!
   295	    def pluck(*column_names)
   296	      if @none
   297	        if @async
   298	          return Promise::Complete.new([])
   299	        else
   300	          return []
   301	        end
   302	      end
   303	
   304	      if loaded? && all_attributes?(column_names)
   305	        result = records.pluck(*column_names)
   306	        if @async
   307	          return Promise::Complete.new(result)
   308	        else
   309	          return result
   310	        end
   311	      end
   312	
   313	      if has_include?(column_names.first)
   314	        relation = apply_join_dependency
   315	        relation.pluck(*column_names)
   316	      else
   317	        model.disallow_raw_sql!(flattened_args(column_names))
   318	        relation = spawn
   319	        columns = relation.arel_columns(column_names)
   320	        relation.select_values = columns
   321	        result = skip_query_cache_if_necessary do
   322	          if where_clause.contradiction? && !possible_aggregation?(column_names)
   323	            ActiveRecord::Result.empty(async: @async)
   324	          else
   325	            model.with_connection do |c|
   326	              c.select_all(relation.arel, "#{model.name} Pluck", async: @async)
   327	            end
   328	          end
   329	        end
   330	        result.then do |result|
   331	          type_cast_pluck_values(result, columns)
   332	        end
   333	      end
   334	    end
   335	
   336	    # Same as #pluck, but performs the query asynchronously and returns an
   337	    # ActiveRecord::Promise.
   338	    def async_pluck(*column_names)
   339	      async.pluck(*column_names)
   340	    end
   341	
   342	    # Pick the value(s) from the named column(s) in the current relation.
   343	    # This is short-hand for <tt>relation.limit(1).pluck(*column_names).first</tt>, and is primarily useful
   344	    # when you have a relation that's already narrowed down to a single row.
   345	    #
   346	    # Just like #pluck, #pick will only load the actual value, not the entire record object, so it's also
   347	    # more efficient. The value is, again like with pluck, typecast by the column type.
   348	    #
   349	    #   Person.where(id: 1).pick(:name)
   350	    #   # SELECT people.name FROM people WHERE id = 1 LIMIT 1
   351	    #   # => 'David'
   352	    #
   353	    #   Person.where(id: 1).pick(:name, :email_address)
   354	    #   # SELECT people.name, people.email_address FROM people WHERE id = 1 LIMIT 1
   355	    #   # => [ 'David', 'david@loudthinking.com' ]
   356	    def pick(*column_names)
   357	      if loaded? && all_attributes?(column_names)
   358	        result = records.pick(*column_names)
   359	        return @async ? Promise::Complete.new(result) : result
   360	      end
   361	
   362	      limit(1).pluck(*column_names).then(&:first)
   363	    end
   364	
   365	    # Same as #pick, but performs the query asynchronously and returns an
   366	    # ActiveRecord::Promise.
   367	    def async_pick(*column_names)
   368	      async.pick(*column_names)
   369	    end
   370	
   371	    # Returns the base model's ID's for the relation using the table's primary key
   372	    #
   373	    #   Person.ids # SELECT people.id FROM people
   374	    #   Person.joins(:company).ids # SELECT people.id FROM people INNER JOIN companies ON companies.id = people.company_id
   375	    def ids
   376	      primary_key_array = Array(primary_key)
   377	
   378	      if loaded?
   379	        result = records.map do |record|
   380	          if primary_key_array.one?
   381	            record._read_attribute(primary_key_array.first)
   382	          else
   383	            primary_key_array.map { |column| record._read_attribute(column) }
   384	          end
   385	        end
   386	        return @async ? Promise::Complete.new(result) : result
   387	      end

   389	      if has_include?(primary_key)
   390	        relation = apply_join_dependency.group(*primary_key_array)

   392	      end
   393	
   394	      columns = arel_columns(primary_key_array)
   395	      relation = spawn
   396	      relation.select_values = columns
   397	
   398	      result = if relation.where_clause.contradiction?
   399	        ActiveRecord::Result.empty
   400	      else
   401	        skip_query_cache_if_necessary do
   402	          model.with_connection do |c|
   403	            c.select_all(relation, "#{model.name} Ids", async: @async)
   404	          end
   405	        end
   406	      end
   407	
   408	      result.then { |result| type_cast_pluck_values(result, columns) }
   409	    end
   410	
   411	    # Same as #ids, but performs the query asynchronously and returns an
   412	    # ActiveRecord::Promise.
   413	    def async_ids
   414	      async.ids
   415	    end

   418	      def aggregate_column(column_name)
   419	        case column_name
   420	        when Arel::Expressions
   421	          column_name
   422	        when :all
   423	          Arel.star
   424	        else
   425	          arel_column(column_name.to_s)
   426	        end
   427	      end

   429	    private
   430	      def all_attributes?(column_names)

     2	
     3	module ActiveRecord
     4	  class AssociationRelation < Relation # :nodoc:
     5	    def initialize(klass, association, **)
     6	      super(klass)
     7	      @association = association
     8	    end
     9	
    10	    def proxy_association
    11	      @association
    12	    end
    13	
    14	    def ==(other)
    15	      other == records
    16	    end
    17	
    18	    %w(insert insert_all insert! insert_all! upsert upsert_all).each do |method|
    19	      class_eval <<~RUBY, __FILE__, __LINE__ + 1
    20	        def #{method}(...)
    21	          if @association.reflection.through_reflection?
    22	            raise ArgumentError, "Bulk insert or upsert is currently not supported for has_many through association"
    23	          end
    24	
    25	          super
    26	        end
    27	      RUBY
    28	    end
    29	
    30	    private
    31	      def _new(attributes, &block)
    32	        @association.build(attributes, &block)
    33	      end
    34	
    35	      def _create(attributes, &block)
    36	        @association.create(attributes, &block)
    37	      end
    38	
    39	      def _create!(attributes, &block)
    40	        @association.create!(attributes, &block)
    41	      end
    42	
    43	      def exec_queries
    44	        super do |record|
    45	          @association.set_inverse_instance_from_queries(record)
    46	          @association.set_strict_loading(record)
    47	          yield record if block_given?
    48	        end
    49	      end
    50	  end
    51	end
     1	# frozen_string_literal: true
     2	
     3	module ActiveRecord
     4	  class DisableJoinsAssociationRelation < Relation # :nodoc:
     5	    attr_reader :ids, :key
     6	
     7	    def initialize(klass, key, ids)
     8	      @ids = ids.uniq
     9	      @key = key
    10	      super(klass)
    11	    end
    12	
    13	    def limit(value)
    14	      records.take(value)
    15	    end
    16	
    17	    def first(limit = nil)
    18	      if limit
    19	        records.limit(limit).first
    20	      else
    21	        records.first
    22	      end
    23	    end
    24	
    25	    def load
    26	      super
    27	      records = @records
    28	
    29	      records_by_id = records.group_by do |record|
    30	        if key.is_a?(Array)
    31	          key.map { |column| record[column] }
    32	        else
    33	          record[key]
    34	        end
    35	      end
    36	
    37	      records = ids.flat_map { |id| records_by_id[id] }
    38	      records.compact!
    39	
    40	      @records = records
    41	    end
    42	  end
    43	end

1	# frozen_string_literal: true
     2	
     3	module ActiveRecord
     4	  module Associations
     5	    # = Active Record Collection Proxy
     6	    #
     7	    # Collection proxies in Active Record are middlemen between an
     8	    # <tt>association</tt>, and its <tt>target</tt> result set.
     9	    #
    10	    # For example, given
    11	    #
    12	    #   class Blog < ActiveRecord::Base
    13	    #     has_many :posts
    14	    #   end
    15	    #
    16	    #   blog = Blog.first
    17	    #
    18	    # The collection proxy returned by <tt>blog.posts</tt> is built from a
    19	    # <tt>:has_many</tt> <tt>association</tt>, and delegates to a collection
    20	    # of posts as the <tt>target</tt>.
    21	    #
    22	    # This class delegates unknown methods to the <tt>association</tt>'s
    23	    # relation class via a delegate cache.
    24	    #
    25	    # The <tt>target</tt> result set is not loaded until needed. For example,
    26	    #
    27	    #   blog.posts.count
    28	    #
    29	    # is computed directly through SQL and does not trigger by itself the
    30	    # instantiation of the actual post records.
    31	    class CollectionProxy < Relation
    32	      def initialize(klass, association, **) # :nodoc:
    33	        @association = association
    34	        super klass
    35	
    36	        extensions = association.extensions
    37	        extend(*extensions) if extensions.any?
    38	      end
    39	
    40	      def target
    41	        @association.target
    42	      end
    43	
    44	      def load_target
    45	        @association.load_target
    46	      end
    47	
    48	      # Returns +true+ if the association has been loaded, otherwise +false+.
    49	      #
    50	      #   person.pets.loaded? # => false
    51	      #   person.pets.records
    52	      #   person.pets.loaded? # => true
    53	      def loaded?
    54	        @association.loaded?
    55	      end
    56	      alias :loaded :loaded?
    57	
    58	      ##
    59	      # :method: select
    60	      #
    61	      # :call-seq:
    62	      #   select(*fields, &block)
    63	      #
    64	      # Works in two ways.
    65	      #
    66	      # *First:* Specify a subset of fields to be selected from the result set.
    67	      #
    68	      #   class Person < ActiveRecord::Base
    69	      #     has_many :pets
    70	      #   end
    71	      #
    72	      #   person.pets
    73	      #   # => [
    74	      #   #       #<Pet id: 1, name: "Fancy-Fancy", person_id: 1>,
    75	      #   #       #<Pet id: 2, name: "Spook", person_id: 1>,
    76	      #   #       #<Pet id: 3, name: "Choo-Choo", person_id: 1>
    77	      #   #    ]
    78	      #
    79	      #   person.pets.select(:name)
    80	      #   # => [
   920	
   921	      # Returns +true+ if the given +record+ is present in the collection.
   922	      #
   923	      #   class Person < ActiveRecord::Base
   924	      #     has_many :pets
   925	      #   end
   926	      #
   927	      #   person.pets # => [#<Pet id: 20, name: "Snoop">]
   928	      #
   929	      #   person.pets.include?(Pet.find(20)) # => true
   930	      #   person.pets.include?(Pet.find(21)) # => false
   931	      def include?(record)
   932	        !!@association.include?(record)
   933	      end
   934	
   935	      # Returns the association object for the collection.
   936	      #
   937	      #   class Person < ActiveRecord::Base
   938	      #     has_many :pets
   939	      #   end
   940	      #
   941	      #   person.pets.proxy_association
   942	      #   # => #<ActiveRecord::Associations::HasManyAssociation owner="#<Person:0x00>">
   943	      #
   944	      # Returns the same object as <tt>person.association(:pets)</tt>,
   945	      # allowing you to make calls like <tt>person.pets.proxy_association.owner</tt>.
   946	      #
   947	      # See Associations::ClassMethods@Association+extensions for more.
   948	      def proxy_association
   949	        @association
   950	      end
   951	
   952	      # Returns a Relation object for the records in this association
   953	      def scope
   954	        @scope ||= @association.scope
   955	      end
   956	
   957	      # Equivalent to <tt>Array#==</tt>. Returns +true+ if the two arrays
   958	      # contain the same number of elements and if each element is equal
   959	      # to the corresponding element in the +other+ array, otherwise returns
   960	      # +false+.
   961	      #
   962	      #   class Person < ActiveRecord::Base
   963	      #     has_many :pets
   964	      #   end
   965	      #
   966	      #   person.pets
   967	      #   # => [
   968	      #   #      #<Pet id: 1, name: "Fancy-Fancy", person_id: 1>,
   969	      #   #      #<Pet id: 2, name: "Spook", person_id: 1>
   970	      #   #    ]
   971	      #
   972	      #   other = person.pets.to_ary
   973	      #
   974	      #   person.pets == other
   975	      #   # => true
   976	      #
   977	      #
   978	      # Note that unpersisted records can still be seen as equal:
   979	      #
   980	      #   other = [Pet.new(id: 1), Pet.new(id: 2)]
   981	      #
   982	      #   person.pets == other
   983	      #   # => true
   984	      def ==(other)
   985	        load_target == other
   986	      end
   987	
   988	      ##
   989	      # :method: to_ary
   990	      #
   991	      # :call-seq:
   992	      #   to_ary()
   993	      #
   994	      # Returns a new array of objects from the collection. If the collection
   995	      # hasn't been loaded, it fetches the records from the database.
   996	      #
   997	      #   class Person < ActiveRecord::Base
   998	      #     has_many :pets
   999	      #   end
  1000	      #
  1001	      #   person.pets
  1002	      #   # => [
  1003	      #   #       #<Pet id: 4, name: "Benny", person_id: 1>,
  1004	      #   #       #<Pet id: 5, name: "Brain", person_id: 1>,
  1005	      #   #       #<Pet id: 6, name: "Boss",  person_id: 1>
  1006	      #   #    ]
  1007	      #
  1008	      #   other_pets = person.pets.to_ary
  1009	      #   # => [
  1010	      #   #       #<Pet id: 4, name: "Benny", person_id: 1>,
  1011	      #   #       #<Pet id: 5, name: "Brain", person_id: 1>,
  1012	      #   #       #<Pet id: 6, name: "Boss",  person_id: 1>
  1013	      #   #    ]
  1014	      #
  1015	      #   other_pets.replace([Pet.new(name: 'BooGoo')])
  1016	      #
  1017	      #   other_pets
  1018	      #   # => [#<Pet id: nil, name: "BooGoo", person_id: 1>]
  1019	      #
  1020	      #   person.pets
  1021	      #   # This is not affected by replace
  1022	      #   # => [
  1023	      #   #       #<Pet id: 4, name: "Benny", person_id: 1>,
  1024	      #   #       #<Pet id: 5, name: "Brain", person_id: 1>,
  1025	      #   #       #<Pet id: 6, name: "Boss",  person_id: 1>
  1026	      #   #    ]
  1027	
  1028	      def records # :nodoc:
  1029	        load_target
  1030	      end
  1031	
  1032	      # Adds one or more +records+ to the collection by setting their foreign keys
  1033	      # to the association's primary key. Since <tt><<</tt> flattens its argument list and
  1034	      # inserts each record, +push+ and +concat+ behave identically. Returns +self+
  1035	      # so several appends may be chained together.
  1036	      #
  1037	      #   class Person < ActiveRecord::Base
  1038	      #     has_many :pets
  1039	      #   end
  1040	      #
  1041	      #   person.pets.size # => 0
  1042	      #   person.pets << Pet.new(name: 'Fancy-Fancy')
  1043	      #   person.pets << [Pet.new(name: 'Spook'), Pet.new(name: 'Choo-Choo')]
  1044	      #   person.pets.size # => 3
  1045	      #
  1046	      #   person.id # => 1
  1047	      #   person.pets
  1048	      #   # => [
  1049	      #   #      #<Pet id: 1, name: "Fancy-Fancy", person_id: 1>,
  1050	      #   #      #<Pet id: 2, name: "Spook", person_id: 1>,
  1051	      #   #      #<Pet id: 3, name: "Choo-Choo", person_id: 1>
  1052	      #   #    ]
  1053	      def <<(*records)
  1054	        proxy_association.concat(records) && self
  1055	      end
  1056	      alias_method :push, :<<
  1057	      alias_method :append, :<<
  1058	      alias_method :concat, :<<
  1059	
  1060	      def prepend(*args) # :nodoc:
  1061	        raise NoMethodError, "prepend on association is not defined. Please use <<, push or append"
  1062	      end
  1063	
  1064	      # Equivalent to +delete_all+. The difference is that returns +self+, instead
  1065	      # of an array with the deleted objects, so methods can be chained. See
  1066	      # +delete_all+ for more information.
  1067	      # Note that because +delete_all+ removes records by directly
  1068	      # running an SQL query into the database, the +updated_at+ column of
  1069	      # the object is not changed.

The association path is clear: association collections are Relation subclasses/proxies, and association scopes are built by creating and merging relations. I’m now checking the scattered dependents the prompt calls out explicitly: bind compilation, cache keys, encryption rewrites, signed IDs, join dependency, named scopes, and cross-gem consumers.

     3	module ActiveRecord
     4	  module Encryption
     5	    # Automatically expand encrypted arguments to support querying both encrypted and unencrypted data
     6	    #
     7	    # Active Record \Encryption supports querying the db using deterministic attributes. For example:
     8	    #
     9	    #   Contact.find_by(email_address: "jorge@hey.com")
    10	    #
    11	    # The value "jorge@hey.com" will get encrypted automatically to perform the query. But there is
    12	    # a problem while the data is being encrypted. This won't work. During that time, you need these
    13	    # queries to be:
    14	    #
    15	    #   Contact.find_by(email_address: [ "jorge@hey.com", "<encrypted jorge@hey.com>" ])
    16	    #
    17	    # This patches ActiveRecord to support this automatically. It addresses both:
    18	    #
    19	    # * ActiveRecord::Base - Used in <tt>Contact.find_by_email_address(...)</tt>
    20	    # * ActiveRecord::Relation - Used in <tt>Contact.internal.find_by_email_address(...)</tt>
    21	    #
    22	    # This module is included if `config.active_record.encryption.extend_queries` is `true`.
    23	    module ExtendedDeterministicQueries
    24	      def self.install_support
    25	        # ActiveRecord::Base relies on ActiveRecord::Relation (ActiveRecord::QueryMethods) but it does
    26	        # some prepared statements caching. That's why we need to intercept +ActiveRecord::Base+ as soon
    27	        # as it's invoked (so that the proper prepared statement is cached).
    28	        ActiveRecord::Relation.prepend(RelationQueries)
    29	        ActiveRecord::Base.include(CoreQueries)
    30	        ActiveRecord::Encryption::EncryptedAttributeType.prepend(ExtendedEncryptableType)
    31	      end
    32	
    33	      # When modifying this file run performance tests in
    34	      # +activerecord/test/cases/encryption/performance/extended_deterministic_queries_performance_test.rb+
    35	      # to make sure performance overhead is acceptable.
    36	      #
    37	      # @TODO We will extend this to support previous "encryption context" versions in future iterations
    38	      # @TODO Experimental. Support for every kind of query is pending
    39	      # @TODO It should not patch anything if not needed (no previous schemes or no support for previous encryption schemes)
    40

    42	        class << self
    43	          def process_arguments(owner, args, check_for_additional_values)
    44	            owner = owner.model if owner.is_a?(Relation)
    45	
    46	            return args if owner.deterministic_encrypted_attributes&.empty?
    47	
    48	            if args.is_a?(Array) && (options = args.first).is_a?(Hash)
    49	              options = options.transform_keys do |key|
    50	                if key.is_a?(Array)
    51	                  key.map(&:to_s)
    52	                else
    53	                  key.to_s
    54	                end
    55	              end
    56	              args[0] = options
    57	
    58	              owner.deterministic_encrypted_attributes&.each do |attribute_name|
    59	                attribute_name = attribute_name.to_s
    60	                type = owner.type_for_attribute(attribute_name)
    61	                if !type.previous_types.empty? && value = options[attribute_name]
    62	                  options[attribute_name] = process_encrypted_query_argument(value, check_for_additional_values, type)
    63	                end
    64	              end
    65	            end
    66	
    67	            args
    68	          end
    69	
    70	          private
    71	            def process_encrypted_query_argument(value, check_for_additional_values, type)
    72	              return value if check_for_additional_values && value.is_a?(Array) && value.last.is_a?(AdditionalValue)
    73	
    74	              case value
    75	              when String, Array
    76	                list = Array(value)
    77	                list + list.flat_map do |each_value|
    78	                  if check_for_additional_values && each_value.is_a?(AdditionalValue)
    79	                    each_value
    80	                  else
    81	                    additional_values_for(each_value, type)
    82	                  end
    83	                end
    84	              else
    85	                value
    86	              end
    87	            end
    88	
    89	            def additional_values_for(value, type)
    90	              type.previous_types.collect do |additional_type|
    91	                AdditionalValue.new(value, additional_type)
    92	              end
    93	            end
    94	        end
    95	      end

   152	        def execute_sql(connection:, sql:, page:, per:)
   153	          unless sql.gsub(/--.*$|\/\*.*?\*\//m, "").match?(/\bLIMIT\b/i)

   165	        def execute_expression(expression:, page:, per:)
   166	          result = with_readonly_connection { eval(expression, TOPLEVEL_BINDING, "(query)", 1) }
   167	          columns, rows, sql, truncated = tabular_result_parts_for(result, expression: expression, page: page, per: per)
   168

   170	        end
   171	
   172	        def tabular_result_parts_for(result, expression:, page:, per:)
   173	          case result
   174	          when ActiveRecord::Relation
   175	            relation = result.offset((page - 1) * per).limit(per + 1)
   176	            relation_sql = relation.to_sql
   177	
   178	            with_readonly_connection_for(relation.model.connection_class_for_self) do |connection|
   179	              active_record_result = connection.select_all(relation_sql)
   180	              rows = active_record_result.rows
   181	
   182	              [ active_record_result.columns, rows.first(per), relation_sql, rows.length > per ]
   183	            end
   184	          when ActiveRecord::Result
   185	            [ result.columns, result.rows, expression, false ]
   186	          when ActiveRecord::Base
   187	            attributes = result.attributes
   188	
   189	            [ attributes.keys, [ attributes.values ], expression, false ]
   190	          when Hash

   222	    end
   223	
   224	    if found
   225	      result
   226	    else
   227	      raise SoleItemExpectedError, "no item found"
   228	    end
   229	  end

   672	    # go through Active Record's type casting and serialization.

     4	  # Statement cache is used to cache a single statement in order to avoid creating the AST again.
     5	  # Initializing the cache is done by passing the statement in the create block:
     6	  #
     7	  #   cache = StatementCache.create(ClothingItem.lease_connection) do |params|
     8	  #     Book.where(name: "my book").where("author_id > 3")
     9	  #   end
    10	  #
    11	  # The cached statement is executed by using the
    12	  # {connection.execute}[rdoc-ref:ConnectionAdapters::DatabaseStatements#execute] method:
    13	  #
    14	  #   cache.execute([], ClothingItem.lease_connection)
    15	  #
    16	  # The relation returned by the block is cached, and for each
    17	  # {execute}[rdoc-ref:ConnectionAdapters::DatabaseStatements#execute]
    18	  # call the cached relation gets duped. Database is queried when +to_a+ is called on the relation.
    19	  #
    20	  # If you want to cache the statement without the values you can use the +bind+ method of the
    21	  # block parameter.
    22	  #
    23	  #   cache = StatementCache.create(ClothingItem.lease_connection) do |params|
    24	  #     Book.where(name: params.bind)
    25	  #   end

   133	      def scope_relation(record, relation)
